title: Fix routing JSON regex for nested braces
analysis:
Both classifier.py (line 144) and router.py (line 163) use `re.search(r'\{[^}]+\}', raw, re.DOTALL)` to extract JSON from LLM responses. This regex matches from the first `{` to the first `}`, so any nested braces in the JSON (e.g., in a reasoning field like `"chose X because {...}"`) cause it to capture an incomplete fragment. `json.loads()` then fails and the code silently falls back to defaults.

The fix: create a shared `extract_json()` utility using brace-counting (finds first `{`, scans forward incrementing/decrementing a counter, returns the balanced substring). This handles arbitrary nesting depth, unlike a nested-regex approach which only handles fixed depth. The utility also strips markdown code fences before scanning, since LLMs commonly wrap JSON in ```json...``` blocks.

Both classifier.py and router.py will import and use this shared utility, replacing the broken regex. Tests cover: nested braces, markdown fences, multiple JSON objects, no JSON present, and integration through the LLM paths of both classifier and router.

steps.description: Create test_json_extract.py with failing tests for extract_json(): (1) simple flat JSON, (2) JSON with nested braces in a value, (3) JSON wrapped in markdown code fences, (4) text with multiple JSON objects (should extract first complete one), (5) no JSON returns None, (6) text before/after JSON is ignored. Import from fused_memory.routing.json_extract.

Create json_extract.py in routing/ with `extract_json(text: str) -> str | None`. Implementation: strip markdown code fences (```json...``` or ```...```), then scan for first `{`, count braces (incrementing on `{`, decrementing on `}`; skip characters inside string literals to handle escaped braces), return substring when count reaches 0. Return None if no balanced JSON found. All step-1 tests pass.

Add tests to test_classifier.py: a new TestLLMClassification class that mocks the OpenAI client. Test (1) `_llm_classify` with a response containing nested braces in reasoning field produces correct ClassificationResult, (2) response wrapped in markdown code fences parses correctly, (3) response with no JSON falls back to default.

Update classifier.py line 144: replace `re.search(r'\{[^}]+\}', raw, re.DOTALL)` with `extract_json(raw)`. Import extract_json from fused_memory.routing.json_extract. Adjust the json_match usage: `json_str = extract_json(raw)` then `if not json_str:` guard, then `data = json.loads(json_str)`. Remove unused `re` import if no longer needed (it's still used for _CATEGORY_PATTERNS). All step-3 tests pass.

Add tests to test_router.py: a new TestLLMRouting class that mocks the OpenAI client. Test (1) `_llm_route` with a response containing nested braces produces correct ReadRouteResult, (2) response wrapped in markdown code fences parses correctly, (3) response with no JSON falls back to broad default.

Update router.py line 163: replace `re.search(r'\{[^}]+\}', raw, re.DOTALL)` with `extract_json(raw)`. Import extract_json from fused_memory.routing.json_extract. Adjust usage same pattern as classifier. All step-5 tests pass.

design_decisions.decision: Extract to a shared json_extract.py module rather than duplicating the fix inline
design_decisions.rationale: The identical regex appears in both classifier.py and router.py. A shared utility eliminates duplication and provides a single place to test the extraction logic thoroughly. Placed in the routing/ package since it's specific to LLM response parsing in that layer.

design_decisions.decision: Use brace-counting with string-literal awareness instead of a nested regex
design_decisions.rationale: A regex like r'\{[^{}]*(?:\{[^{}]*\}[^{}]*)*\}' only handles one level of nesting. Brace-counting handles arbitrary depth. String-literal tracking (skipping braces inside quotes) handles edge cases like escaped braces in JSON string values. This is more robust for real LLM output.

design_decisions.decision: Strip markdown code fences in the extract_json utility
design_decisions.rationale: LLMs frequently wrap JSON in ```json...``` blocks. Handling this in the shared utility means both classifier and router get this resilience for free, and it's tested in one place.

design_decisions.decision: Return str | None from extract_json rather than re.Match
design_decisions.rationale: The callers only need the JSON string to pass to json.loads(). Returning the string directly is a simpler API than returning a Match object, and decouples the implementation from regex.

reuse.how: Both test_classifier.py and test_router.py already use this fixture. New LLM-path tests will use it too, enabling the classifier/router with llm_fallback=False by default, and overriding to True for specific LLM tests.

The project uses AsyncMock extensively. New LLM-path tests will mock the OpenAI completions.create call using the same pattern: create an AsyncMock that returns a mock response object with choices[0].message.content set to the test LLM output string.
The fix doesn't change the model layer — the same result types are returned. Tests assert against these existing model types.
